title: The Black Death
subject: English
description: Key terms, dates, causes, symptoms, beliefs, treatments and consequences for KS3 revision on the Black Death

translations.en-GB: A severe outbreak of bubonic plague that affected England from 1348 to 1350.

data.targetWord: A severe outbreak of bubonic plague that affected England from 1348 to 1350.

translations.en-GB: A deadly disease that caused fever, coughing and painful swellings called buboes.

data.targetWord: A deadly disease that caused fever, coughing and painful swellings called buboes.

translations.en-GB: Small parasites that carried plague bacteria and spread the disease by biting people.

data.targetWord: Small parasites that carried plague bacteria and spread the disease by biting people.

translations.en-GB: Rats that carried infected fleas on ships and in towns.

translations.en-GB: A medieval belief that poisonous or foul-smelling air spread disease.

data.targetWord: A medieval belief that poisonous or foul-smelling air spread disease.

translations.en-GB: The idea that disease was caused by bad air or foul smells.

translations.en-GB: Primitive living conditions and dirty streets that helped disease spread.

translations.en-GB: The inability to read, which meant many people had little access to written medical knowledge.

translations.en-GB: Changes to society such as abandoned villages, movement to the countryside and attacks on minorities.

translations.en-GB: Land left unfarmed because there were fewer peasants to work it.

translations.en-GB: Many people questioned the Church because priests and monks died despite prayer.

translations.en-GB: The plague returned roughly every decade, reducing England’s population over the next century.

translations.en-GB: King of England during the outbreak; his daughter Joan died of the disease.

translations.en-GB: The disease spread from ports across England within a year.

data.targetWord: The disease spread from ports across England within a year.

translations.en-GB: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of bubonic plague in England from 1348 to 1350.

data.sourceSentence: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of disease in England from 1348 to 1350.
data.targetSentence: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of bubonic plague in England from 1348 to 1350.

translations.en-GB: The real cause was bubonic plague, spread by infected fleas that lived on rats.

data.sourceSentence: Explain the real cause of the Black Death.
data.targetSentence: The real cause was bubonic plague, spread by infected fleas that lived on rats.

translations.en-GB: The Black Death first reached England at Melcombe Regis in Dorset in June 1348.

data.sourceSentence: The Black Death first reached England at ___.
data.targetSentence: The Black Death first reached England at Melcombe Regis in Dorset in June 1348.

translations.en-GB: Ports helped the plague spread because infected rats and fleas travelled on ships.

data.sourceSentence: Why did ports help the plague spread?
data.targetSentence: Ports helped the plague spread because infected rats and fleas travelled on ships.

translations.en-GB: One reason the plague spread quickly was that trade and shipping linked many towns and ports.

data.sourceSentence: One reason the plague spread quickly was ___.
data.targetSentence: One reason the plague spread quickly was that trade and shipping linked many towns and ports.

translations.en-GB: Early symptoms of the Black Death included sweating, coughing and fever.

data.sourceSentence: Early symptoms included sweating and coughing.
data.targetSentence: Early symptoms of the Black Death included sweating, coughing and fever.

translations.en-GB: Buboes were large painful lumps that appeared on victims’ bodies, often on the arms and legs.

data.sourceSentence: What were buboes?
data.targetSentence: Buboes were large painful lumps that appeared on victims’ bodies, often on the arms and legs.

translations.en-GB: Some lumps turned black because blood dried out inside the body.

data.sourceSentence: Why did some lumps turn black?
data.targetSentence: Some lumps turned black because blood dried out inside the body.

translations.en-GB: Most people who caught the Black Death died within five days.

data.sourceSentence: Most victims died within ___.
data.targetSentence: Most people who caught the Black Death died within five days.

translations.en-GB: Many medieval people believed the plague was caused by bad air, also called miasma.

data.sourceSentence: Medieval people often blamed bad air.
data.targetSentence: Many medieval people believed the plague was caused by bad air, also called miasma.

translations.en-GB: Many people believed the Black Death was a punishment from God.

data.sourceSentence: Explain the religious explanation for the plague.
data.targetSentence: Many people believed the Black Death was a punishment from God.

translations.en-GB: Some people prayed more because they believed God could save them from the disease.

data.sourceSentence: Why did some people pray more during the plague?
data.targetSentence: Some people prayed more because they believed God could save them from the disease.

translations.en-GB: Doctors were unable to cure the Black Death because they did not understand bacteria, fleas or infection.

data.sourceSentence: Why were doctors unable to cure the Black Death?
data.targetSentence: Doctors were unable to cure the Black Death because they did not understand bacteria, fleas or infection.

translations.en-GB: Some people used dried flowers, herbs, toads, leeches, figs or butter as attempted treatments.

data.sourceSentence: Name one attempted treatment.
data.targetSentence: Some people used dried flowers, herbs, toads, leeches, figs or butter as attempted treatments.

translations.en-GB: The Black Death killed about one-third of England’s population, around 1.5 million people.

data.sourceSentence: The Black Death killed about one-third of England’s population.
data.targetSentence: The Black Death killed about one-third of England’s population, around 1.5 million people.

translations.en-GB: Many villages were abandoned because so many people died or moved away.

data.sourceSentence: How did the Black Death affect villages?
data.targetSentence: Many villages were abandoned because so many people died or moved away.

translations.en-GB: Some Jews were wrongly blamed for causing the disease, so they were attacked or forced into separate areas.

data.sourceSentence: Why were some Jews attacked?
data.targetSentence: Some Jews were wrongly blamed for causing the disease, so they were attacked or forced into separate areas.

translations.en-GB: With fewer peasants, agricultural land was unused and livestock were left to die.

data.sourceSentence: How did the Black Death affect farming?
data.targetSentence: With fewer peasants, agricultural land was unused and livestock were left to die.

translations.en-GB: Food prices rose because fewer workers meant lower food supplies.

data.sourceSentence: Why did food prices rise?
data.targetSentence: Food prices rose because fewer workers meant lower food supplies.

translations.en-GB: Workers could demand higher wages because there were fewer peasants and craftsmen available.

data.sourceSentence: Why could workers ask for higher wages?
data.targetSentence: Workers could demand higher wages because there were fewer peasants and craftsmen available.

translations.en-GB: Lords offered villeins more food and money to work their land.

data.sourceSentence: How did lords respond to labour shortages?
data.targetSentence: Lords offered villeins more food and money to work their land.

translations.en-GB: The Ordinance of Labourers was a 1349 law that tried to keep wages at pre-plague levels.

data.sourceSentence: What was the Ordinance of Labourers?
data.targetSentence: The Ordinance of Labourers was a 1349 law that tried to keep wages at pre-plague levels.

translations.en-GB: Many people lost faith because priests, monks and those who prayed also died.

data.sourceSentence: Why did many people lose faith in the Church?
data.targetSentence: Many people lost faith because priests, monks and those who prayed also died.

translations.en-GB: The plague returned roughly every 10 years and England’s population declined over the following century.

data.sourceSentence: What happened in the next 100 years?
data.targetSentence: The plague returned roughly every 10 years and England’s population declined over the following century.

translations.en-GB: The Black Death helped increase tensions over wages, labour duties and taxes before the Peasants’ Revolt of 1381.

data.sourceSentence: How was the Black Death linked to the Peasants’ Revolt?
data.targetSentence: The Black Death helped increase tensions over wages, labour duties and taxes before the Peasants’ Revolt of 1381.

translations.en-GB: It changed population, wages, villages, religious attitudes and the relationship between peasants and lords.

data.sourceSentence: Why is the Black Death historically significant?
data.targetSentence: It changed population, wages, villages, religious attitudes and the relationship between peasants and lords.

translations.en-GB: A better explanation is that infected fleas living on rats spread the bubonic plague.

data.sourceSentence: A common misconception is that rats alone caused the plague.
data.targetSentence: A better explanation is that infected fleas living on rats spread the bubonic plague.

translations.en-GB: It showed that medieval medicine was based more on beliefs and observation than scientific understanding.

data.sourceSentence: What did the plague reveal about medieval medicine?
data.targetSentence: It showed that medieval medicine was based more on beliefs and observation than scientific understanding.
